skill.name: git-workspace-init
skill.description: Initialize a Git worktree with a conventional branch name for feature work, fixes, documentation, refactors, and other isolated tasks.
skill.summary: Creates an isolated Git workspace with a named branch and optional remote tracking.
skill.author: BillChirico

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command patterns, weak cryptography alerts, filesystem access, and one hardcoded URL. Review found no prompt injection, credential handling, malware intent, or data exfiltration. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill instructs agents to run Git commands that create worktrees, fetch from remotes, push branches, and write a .worktrees directory.

medium_findings.title: Git Commands Modify Local and Remote Repository State
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create worktrees, fetch from origin, and push new branches. This matches the skill purpose, but it can change repository state and contact configured remotes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Git operations are explicit and semantically aligned with the skill. They are not malicious, but they perform real local and remote repository actions.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation Link Is Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to public conventional branch naming documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends data to this URL or performs a network request to it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only as a Markdown reference in prose. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or data transmission tied to it.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alerts occur in documentation about branch names, task examples, and Git error text. No cryptographic API, hash algorithm call, or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found branch-naming guidance and example text at the flagged locations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python subprocess.run Invokes Git
dangerous_patterns.description: Example Python workflows call Git through subprocess.run. Arguments are structured arrays, which reduces shell injection risk, but the commands still fetch, create branches, and push to remotes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit and use Git commands. The array form and branch-name normalization reduce injection risk, but repository side effects remain.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Examples Create and Clean Worktrees
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash examples use Git commands for worktree creation, push setup, listing, removal, and pruning. These are expected operations but should run only in the intended repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented clearly and perform normal Git operations. Risk depends on the active repository and user authorization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes Under .worktrees
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill creates a .worktrees directory under the repository root and creates worktree paths inside it. This is expected, but it writes directories on the local filesystem.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are explicit and limited to the repository .worktrees location. No evidence found of hidden file scraping or writes outside the repository root.

content.value_statement: Developers need clean workspaces when they start features, fixes, or documentation changes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through branch naming, worktree creation, and remote tracking.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through selecting a conventional branch type.
Normalizes task descriptions into lowercase hyphenated branch names.
Creates a .worktrees workspace path based on the branch name.
Shows Git commands for creating a worktree from origin/main.
Shows how to push the new branch with upstream tracking.
Provides cleanup commands for removing worktrees after merge.
limitations: Requires an existing Git repository with a configured remote.
Assumes the base branch is main unless the user changes it.
Requires permission to create branches and push to origin.
Does not create pull requests or merge completed work.

use_cases.title: Start Feature Development
use_cases.description: Create a dedicated worktree and branch before implementing a new product feature.

use_cases.title: Isolate Bug Fixes
use_cases.description: Set up a separate branch and folder for a bug fix without disturbing current work.

use_cases.title: Prepare Documentation Changes
use_cases.description: Create a docs branch and worktree for reference updates, guides, or changelog work.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Workspace
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Git worktree for a feature named user authentication. Use the default base branch.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Fix Workspace
prompt_templates.scenario: Bug fix with a clear branch name.
prompt_templates.prompt: Initialize a worktree for a fix called login validation error. Show the branch name and workspace path before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Custom Base Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Team workflow that does not branch from main.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a hotfix worktree for payment timeout using release/2026.06 as the base branch. Confirm the remote first.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Parallel Worktrees
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with multiple isolated tasks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan worktrees for a refactor, a test update, and a docs update. Recommend branch names and identify any conflicts before creating them.

output_examples.input: Create a worktree for a feature called dark mode.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes the branch feat/dark-mode, creates a matching workspace under .worktrees, and reports the path.

output_examples.input: Start a bug fix for checkout rounding errors.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects the fix type, creates fix/checkout-rounding-errors, pushes it to origin, and explains the next navigation step.

output_examples.input: Clean up after my feature branch was merged.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists the relevant worktree cleanup steps and branch deletion checks without removing unrelated workspaces.

best_practices: Confirm the target repository and base branch before creating a worktree.
Use short branch names that describe one task only.
Review Git commands before pushing branches to a shared remote.
anti_patterns: Do not create worktrees from the wrong repository root.
Do not reuse a branch name for unrelated work.
Do not remove a worktree before confirming its changes are saved.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a new Git worktree folder and a matching branch for one task.

faq.question: Does it push branches to a remote?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow includes pushing the new branch to origin with upstream tracking.

faq.question: Can I use a base branch other than main?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the base branch name when starting the workspace.

faq.question: Does it modify source files?
faq.answer: No. It creates Git workspace structure, but it does not edit application code.

faq.question: Is this safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it only after confirming the repository, branch name, and remote. It can create and push branches.

faq.question: How do I remove a workspace later?
faq.answer: Use the documented Git worktree removal and pruning commands after confirming the branch is merged or no longer needed.
